title: Base’s Supply Side Growth
text:
Base is a protocol, a smartcontract platform based on Optimism Stack. We can also view it as a marketplace which has 2 sides: supply and demand.
On the demand side are users, who use smartcontracts to achieve their goals in the decentralized world. And on the supply side are developers, who build those smartcontracts.
Growing a marketplace needs both sides to grow. It’s always better to grow fast, especially in the early days like an 1-month old Base.
As you can see at the title, in this document, I will examine the supply side growth of Base. I started doing this with a question in mind: “What metrics would I want to see analyzed if I was the Base team?”. The demand side is as important as the supply side, however, at the testnet phase, I think examining the supply side is more valuable.
But first, let’s briefly look at Base testnet processing capacity such as transactions, fail/success rate, and success transaction by transaction type.






Tip



All charts below are interactive which you can scroll/click/drag/hover to change scale, zoom in/out, or view detail. This is very helpful when reading charts, especially reading charts which have outliers and log scale doesn’t add much value, thus hard to see normal data points without interactive mode.

title: Base’s Supply Side Growth
text:

On the demand side are users, who use smartcontracts to achieve their goals in the decentralized world. And on the supply side are developers, who build those smartcontracts.
Growing a marketplace needs both sides to grow. It’s always better to grow fast, especially in the early days like an 1-month old Base.
As you can see at the title, in this document, I will examine the supply side growth of Base. I started doing this with a question in mind: “What metrics would I want to see analyzed if I was the Base team?”. The demand side is as important as the supply side, however, at the testnet phase, I think examining the supply side is more valuable.
But first, let’s briefly look at Base testnet processing capacity such as transactions, fail/success rate, and success transaction by transaction type.






Tip



All charts below are interactive which you can scroll/click/drag/hover to change scale, zoom in/out, or view detail. This is very helpful when reading charts, especially reading charts which have outliers and log scale doesn’t add much value, thus hard to see normal data points without interactive mode.

title: Base’s Supply Side Growth
text:
Processing Capacity




                                                
Figure 1: Total Transactions & Fail Rate Over Time



Since launched on Feb 23. Base processed mostly between 100k and 200k transactions per day and fail rate mostly less than 5%, let’s consider these numbers as baseline. Total transactions peaked on Mar 6 at 564k, and fail rate also peaked at 17%. Since then, both total transactions and faile rate have decreased to the baseline.




                                                
Figure 2: Success Transaction by Type



In total 5.4M success transactions: the shares are 73% normal (by contracts users), 24% system (by Base), 2% deploying contract (by developers).
A developer can deploy a contract several times on Base, each deploy is 1 transactions with unique contract address. Thus using deploying contract transaction numbers to evaluate the supply side growth of Base (or other EVM protocols) is not quite accurate. A more accurate way is by counting interacted contracts (i.e. contract users interacted with).






Why would developer deploy a contract several times?





A developer may deploy a contract several times for various reasons. One reason is to test the contract’s functionality and ensure that it works as intended before deploying it to the main network. Another reason is to create multiple instances of the same contract with different parameters or configurations. Additionally, deploying a new version of a contract can be necessary if there are bugs or security vulnerabilities in the previous version that need to be fixed. Finally, developers may deploy contracts multiple times for auditing purposes or to compare different versions of the same contract.

title: Base’s Supply Side Growth
text:
Supply Side Growth

Contracts Over Time




                                                
Figure 3: Aggregate Contracts Over Time




Deployed Contracts

count contracts at deployed time

Interacted Contracts

count contracts at first time interacted by any user

Adopted Contracts

count contracts at first time interacted by any user who is not the contract creator


Deployed contracts has been growing much faster than both interacted contracts and adopted contracts. Base currently has 130K deployed contracts, 12K interacted contracts, and almost 1K adopted contracts. Each smaller level is 10x lesser than the bigger one.


Contracts Conversion Over Time




                                                
Figure 4: Contracts Conversion Rate Over Time



The conversion rate from deployed to interacted started with 88% on Feb 23, and then dropped sharply to 28% on Feb 27. Since then, it has decreased gradually to 9% recently.
The conversion rate from interacted to adopted mostly plateau, started with 6% and recently at around 9%.
From 2 conversion rates above, it can be summarized as for every 10 deployed contracts, only 1 got interacted by any user, and for every 10 interacted contracts, only 1 got adopted by normal users.
To close the big conversion gap from deployed to interacted to adopted, it needs more effort growing the demand side of the Base Growth team.






Note



You might wonder why there are 117K deploy contract transaction, but total contracts deployed is 124K. The reason is 1 deploy contract transaction may have more than 1 contract deployed.




Developers Over Time






Note



With developers, I don’t count interacted level because interaction of a developer with their own contracts doesn’t add signal to their contracts true adoption.






                                                
Figure 5: Aggregate Developers Over Time




Deployed Devs

count developers at the time of deploying their first contract

Adopted Devs

count developers at the first time any user interacts with any of their deployed contracts


Similar to contracts, developers who deploy contracts grow much faster than those have contracts adopted by users. As of Mar 24, Base has 37K deployed developers and 282 adopted developers.


Developers Conversion Over Time




                                                
Figure 6: Deployed to Adopted Developers Conversion Rate Over Time



The conversion from deployed to adopted started at 3.7% on Feb 24, then dropped sharply to 0.7% on Mar 01. Since then, it has fluctuated between 0.7% and 1.1%. In other words, for every 100 developers, only 1 sees their work used by normal users.

title: Base’s Supply Side Growth
section: Relationship between Activeness and Contracts of Developers
text:
Relationship between Activeness and Contracts of Developers

Age and Deployed Contracts




                                                
Figure 7: Age and Deployed Contracts (log)




Age

days since the first time deploying a contract


Except for a few outliers, the general trend is the older a developer, the more deployed contracts.
The most common [Age - Deployed Contracts] cluster is [2 weeks old - 1 deployed contracts] which has 9.7K developers.
One 3 weeks old developer has deployed more than 6K contracts or around 300 contracts per day. This is either a super coder, or an AI, or a team using the same address to deploy contract!


Deploy Frequency and Adopted Contracts




                                                
Figure 8: Deploy Frequency and Adopted Contracts




Deploy Frequency

deployed contracts per day = deployed contracts / age


This section is where interactive mode of a chart shines. If you zoom in the cluster at the bottom left corner, you will see that most developers have less than 10 adopted contracts, and they deploy less than 3 contracts per day.
When the deploy frequency is less than 1 contract per day, it looks like there is a positive correlation between deploy frequency and adopted contracts. However, the correlation fades when deploy frequency surpasses 1 contract per day.
There are 2 outlier developers which have quite opposite results, one deploys 26 contracts per day and has 400 adopted contracts, the other deploys 53 contracts per day and only has 9 adopted contracts.

title: Base’s Supply Side Growth
text:
Base Guild.XYZ




                                                
Figure 9: Base Guild.XYZ Members



A quick introduction to Guild.XYZ: Guild is a platform that allows users to create private gated communities. Base team created the Base on the same day of the testnet launch to manage and grow their community. To join the guild, each member must acquires a role by completing some actions.
You can read more about each role here
What I want to focus on is the Shadowy Super Coder role, who stars Base github repository (i.e. the developers). Currently, there are 25K of them and we know in the previous section that Base has around 36K developers who have deployed contracts on Base, which means only 2/3 of Base developer base are aware of the Base Guild on Guild.XYZ.
The goal of having a guild is to build and grow the community of both sides: Demand and Supply. The Base growth team should dig deeper into this and find a way to onboard more developers to the Guild.

title: Base’s Supply Side Growth
text:
Final Thoughts
On the surface, Base supply side seems to be growing very fast with both number of developers who deploy contracts and number of deployed contracts are increasing rapidly. However, taking a closer look, we can see that true usage which represented by number of adopted developers and adopted contracts (i.e used by normal users) is relatively small. Improving the conversion from deployed to adopted is the question for the growth team at Base to answer and I hope this article has provided some insight into which questions should the team ask.

title: Uniswap V3 License Expiry
text:
Since the license expiry, 8 protocols have forked Uniswap V3 to create their own V3. By Apr 30, 2023:

Uniswap V3 has $2.9B in total TVL on 6 chains. The forks have $263M in total TVL or 9% of Uniswap V3 total TVL.
The biggest fork is Pancakeswap V3 with $260M TVL (99% in the forks total TVL).
Pancakeswap V3 is operating on 2 chains: BNB (87% in total Pancakeswap V3 TVL) and Ethereum (13% in Pancakeswap V3 total TVL).
On Ethereum, Pancakeswap V3 has $37M TVL, which is 1.5% of $2.4B Uniswap V3 TVL.
On BNB, Uniswap V3 has $20M TVL (launched on Mar 16, 2023), which is 9% of $223M Pancakeswap V3 TVL.



I will examine onchain data since the Expiry, from Apr 1 to Apr 30.

To examine the impact of the Expiry, I will examine Uniswap V3 performance on 2 chains: Ethereum, and BNB.
To see how the forks are doing relative to Uniswap V3, I will compare Uniswap V3 to Pancakeswap V3 on each of Ethereum and BNB chain. To make the comparison more compatible, I will exclude all Uniswap V3 users before the Expiry (i.e. imagine Uniswap V3 launched after the Expiry, the same as the fork).

title: Uniswap V3 License Expiry
text:
On Ethereum


Value Locked




                                                



                                                


In the first 11 days, there were more increase LP than decrease LP. And by Apr 11, avl was - $78M. Apr 5 saw the most outflow with - $95M in net value locked.
The trend changed on Apr 12, it saw the most inflow with $107M in net value locked. The avl peaked on Apr 21 with $114M then decreased. By Apr 30, avl was $50M.
Meanwhile, only 3 of 30 days have more increase LP than decrease LP. And the trend of both increase LP and decrease LP were sligthly down.


Swap




                                                



                                                


In the first 14 days, the trend of volume was up. It peaked on Apr 14 with $1.3B. Then it decreased. By Apr 30, it was $482M.
Fee and # swapper peaked at the same time (Apr 19 and Apr 20).

Which coins drove the surge in # swapper?



                                                


The surge in # swapper was driven by meme coins such as PEPE, WOJAK, FADE, and MEMEME in their pools with WETH.


Did the meme coins help Uniswap V3 acquire more new swapper than other coins?



                                                


Yes, % new swapper (= # new swapper / total # swapper) in pools of the meme coins is 1.2x - 2.4x higher than in pools of other coins.

title: Uniswap V3 License Expiry
text:
On BNB


Value Locked




                                                



                                                


In most days, the net value locked was positive. A big outflow day was immediately followed by a big inflow day. By Apr 30, the aggreagate value locked was $1.75M. This is much lower than what is showned on Uniswap V3 website. The reasons are:

I exclude BTCBR coin as its’ price data is wrong, which inflates value locked a lot.
The source data I use doesn’t have price data of UITP. The USDT<>UITP TVL is $76M (as shown on Uniswap V3 website), however its 24h volume is only $50. This raises a question that is this fake TVL. How can its 24h volume be so low relative to its TVL. My solution is also excluding UITP from calculating Uniswap V3 TVL on BNB chain.

Unlike on Ethereum, 28 of 30 days have more increase LP than decrease LP.

title: Uniswap V3 License Expiry
text:
Swap




                                                



                                                


Volume, fee, and # swapper basically moved in a range. Volume and fee are more volatile than # swapper, which is healthier than otherwise, in my opinion.
Volume and fee might be affected by coins’ price volatility, which is normal in crypto. While with # swapper, we can have a clearer picture of swap usage.
On Apr 10 and 11, # swapper surged while volume and fee did not.

Which coins drove the surge in # swapper?



                                                


USDC, USDT, and WBNB are 3 coins behind the surge in # swapper on Apr 10, 11.

title: Uniswap V3 License Expiry
text:
Summary

On Ethereum and BNB, impact of the Expiry on Uniswap V3 was small and only observable on value locked in the first few days after the Expiry. From Apr 1 to Apr 30, the general trends in value locked and swap activities were either up or plateau.
During Apr 2023, swap activities on Uniswap V3 saw a few surges. On Ethereum, the surge was driven by meme coins. On BNB, the surge was driven by stablecoins.

title: Uniswap V3 License Expiry
text:
On Ethereum

Value Locked



                                                



                                                


From Apr 1 to Apr 30, Pancakeswap V3 on Ethereum has $27M in aggregate value locked. This number can also be treated as TVL because Pancakeswap V3 launched on Apr 1.
4 days after launch, on Apr 4, Pancakeswap V3 saw the most inflow of value locked per day with $5.5M net value locked. However, value locked inflow lost momentum after that. On Apr 30, Pancakeswap V3 saw the most outflow in value locked per day with - $10M net value locked.
# liquidity provider surged in first 4 days, peaked on Apr 4 with 77 LPs who increased value locked, and 44 LPs who decreased value locked. Since then, the numbers were in downward slopes. By Apr 30, the numbers were 18 increase LPs and 14 decrease LPs.
Most of the time in Apr, there were more LPs who increased their value locked than LPs who decreased their value locked.


Swap



                                                



                                                


During 2 weeks after launch, volume and fee were rising and peaked on Apr 14 with $9.4M in volume and $7K in fee. After that, the numbers decreased. By Apr 30, they are $4.5M in volume and $2.6K in fee.
Unlike volume and fee, # swapper kept rising during the period. On Apr 30, it peaked at 720 # swapper per day.

title: Uniswap V3 License Expiry
text:
On BNB

Value Locked



                                                



                                                


By Apr 30, Pancakeswap V3 on BNB has $214M in aggregate value locked. And the trend is similar to Ethereum, where net value locked per day peaked early on Apr 3, then lost momentum.
# liquidity provider (LP) also peaked early on Apr 4 with 2.3K # LPs who increased value locked, and 1K # LPs who decreased value locked. By Apr 30, the former was down to 987 # LPs and the latter was down to 747 # LPs.
Increase LPs was always greater than decrease LPs during the period.


Swap



                                                



                                                


From Apr 1 to Apr 21, volume and fee were in upward slopes. Volume surpassed $100M on Apr 10, peaked at $200M on Apr 21. After that, it gradually decreased. On Apr 30, it was $138M.
# swapper peaked 5 days later than volume on Apr 26 with 31.5K # swapper. Overall, the trend is up.

title: Uniswap V3 License Expiry
text:
Summary
On Ethereum and BNB:

Value locked acitivities of Pancakeswap V3 saw a good start in the first 4 days. Both net value locked per day and # LP per day peaked on Apr 4. Then they decreased.
Swap activities have a healthier trend, were generally up from Apr 1 to Apr 30.

title: Uniswap V3 License Expiry
text:
On Ethereum

Value Locked




                                                






                                                


By Apr 30, Uniswap V3 grew much faster than Pancakeswap V3 in term of aggregate lp and tvl.

almost 6x in # aggregate lps (2.8K vs. 500).
almost 4x in $ tvl ($104M vs. $27M).



Swap




                                                






                                                


By Apr 30, Uniswap V3 grew much faster than Pancakeswap V3 in term of aggregate swapper and volume.

20x in # aggregate swapper (82K vs. 3.7K).
10x in $ volume per day when comparing highest days ($106M vs. $9.5M).

title: Uniswap V3 License Expiry
text:
On BNB

Value Locked




                                                






                                                


The picture is opposite on BNB. By Apr 30, Pancakeswap V3 grew much faster than Uniswap V3 both in term of aggregate lp and tvl.

almost 20x in # aggregate lp (22K vs. 1.4K).
almost 40x in $ tvl ($224M vs. $6M).



Swap




                                                






                                                


Unlike the situation on Ethereum, by Apr 30, Pancakeswap V3 grew much faster than Uniswap V3 both in term of aggregate swapper and volume.

20x in # aggregate swapper (390K vs. 19K).
20x in $ volume per day when comparing highest days ($200M vs. $9.5M).

title: Uniswap V3 License Expiry
section: Pancakeswap V3 Users who are also Uniswap V3 Users
text:
Pancakeswap V3 Users who are also Uniswap V3 Users




                                                


More than 1/10 of Pancakeswap V3 LP on Ethereum are Uniswap V3 LP. The number is only 3% on BNB.




                                                


More than 1/5 of Pancakeswap V3 Swapper on Ethereum are Uniswap V3 Swapper. The number is only 1% on BNB.

title: Uniswap V3 License Expiry
text:
Summary
Since the Expiry:

On Ethereum, Uniswap V3 is growing much faster than Pancakeswap V3 (up to 4x in tvl and 20x in swap volume).
It’s the opposite on BNB, where Pancakeswap V3 is growing much faster than Uniswap V3 with bigger gap than Ethereum (up to 40x in tvl and 20 x in swap volume).
As Uniswap V3 user base on Ethereum is much larger than on BNB, the overlap between Pancakeswap V3 user base on Ethereum is also much larger than on BNB.

title: Uniswap User Origination
text:
If not specified, data in this post is of all time Uniswap history until July 30, 2023.
If you see error of loading charts, please refresh to reload this page (a few times if necessary).
For general tracking, or if you want to see latest data, please visit this dashboard.
A Defi user (trader) trades in Uniswap pools by either (1) swapping through graphic user interface, i.e. on a DEX website, or (2) directly calling the swap function of a smart contract through API. The former is for normal, every day users, and the latter is for advance users who know smart contract programming language such as Solidity. In either ways, a smart contract is called.
Since launched in early November 2018, Uniswap saw 450K+ contracts called by 11.3M traders to trade $1.6T volume in 276M+ trades in 241K+ pools.


The contracts can be grouped into a few trading routes by referring to contracts’ label supported by popular crypto application such as a block explorer, or a crypto analytic platform. In this post, I use label provided by Dune (the main source), and Etherscan to group the contracts into routes. The routes include (1) Uniswap Frontend, (2) Aggregators, (3) MEV, (4) Oku, and (5) Unknowns.



Uniswap Frontend is Uniswap official application, where normal traders use to execute their trades.
Aggregators is other DEXs’ applications, where normal traders use to execute their trades.
MEV is smart contracts, usually called bots, where advance traders use to execute their trades to exploit MEV.
Oku is an advanced application, backed by Uniswap Foundation, launched in early June 2023, where normal traders can execute market order or limit order. Caveat: a market order from Oku is indistinguishable from a swap on Uniswap Frontend, thus is included in Uniswap Frontend route.
Unknowns is a group of smart contracts, which are not yet labeled.

Caveat: Uniswap Frontend has a few main contracts called router. Most of 1.8K contracts you see in the chart below are Uniswap v1 pools (exchanges in v1 technical term). In Uniswap v1, pools contract are called directly, which makes it hard to differentiate between (1) trade made on Uniswap application and (2) trade made through API. My assumption here is the v1 pools contracts were mainly called by the type (1) trade. Thus I include all of the contracts in Uniswap Frontend.
Number of contract of the routes range between 1 (Oku) and 349K (Aggregators). For every 100 contracts called, 79 in Aggregators, 20 in Unknowns, MEV and Uniswap Frontend roughly equally split the rest.












We have learned the definition about the trading routes, which a trader can take to trade in Uniswap pools. The rest of this post, let’s explore the routes’ role in onboarding trader and growing Uniswap trades.

title: Uniswap User Origination
section: What are the routes’ contribution in onboarding new traders to Uniswap?
text:
What are the routes’ contribution in onboarding new traders to Uniswap?
A trader is counted to a route if the route onboard the trader to Uniswap, i.e. the trader executed the first Uniswap trade on the route.
Number of trader onboarded by the routes range between 2 (Oku) and 6.3M (Uniswap Frontend). For every 100 traders, 55 started with Uniswap Frontend, 35 started with Aggregators, 9 started with Unknowns, and 1 started with MEV.

title: Uniswap User Origination
section: What are the routes’ contribution to total Uniswap volume?
text:
What are the routes’ contribution to total Uniswap volume?
Volume of the routes range between $97K+ (Oku) and $678B+ (MEV). For every $100 in total volume, $42 from MEV, $29 from Uniswap Frontend, $15 from Aggregators, and $14 from Unknowns.

title: Uniswap User Origination
section: Do the routes have traders in common?
text:
Do the routes have traders in common?
Yes, the routes have traders in common, i.e. multi-route traders.
For every 100 traders started with Uniswap Frontend, 26 are multi-route traders.
For every 100 traders started with Aggregators, 23 are multi-route traders.
For every 100 traders started with MEV, 38 are multi-route traders.
For every 100 traders started with Unknowns, 26 are multi-route traders.
All Oku traders are multi-route.



Ages of single route traders and multi-route traders
Of the routes other than Oku, multi-route traders, which average age ranges between 21 weeks (Unknowns) and 40 weeks (Uniswap Frontend), are much older than single route traders, which average age ranges between 3 weeks (MEV) and 8 weeks (Uniswap Frontend).




How many weeks after the first trade, a single route trader become a multi-route trader?
For every 100 multi-route traders, 32 traders became multi-route in the first week (week 0), 31 traders became multi-route between week 1 and week 8, 37 traders became multi-route after week 8.


For every 100 traders who started with Uniswap Frontend, then became multi-route, 29 traders became multi-route in week 0, 28 traders became multi-route between week 1 and week 8, 43 traders became multi-route after week 8.
For every 100 traders who started with Aggregators, then became multi-route, 34 traders became multi-route in week 0, 36 traders became multi-route between week 1 and week 8, 30 traders became multi-route after week 8.
For every 100 traders who started with MEV, then became multi-route, 29 traders became multi-route in week 0, 31 traders became multi-route between week 1 and week 8, 40 traders became multi-route after week 8.
For every 100 traders who started with Unknowns, then became multi-route, 42 traders became multi-route in week 0, 33 traders became multi-route between week 1 and week 8, 25 traders became multi-route after week 8.
Of 2 traders who started with Oku, then became multi-route, 1 traders became multi-route in week 0, 1 traders became multi-route between week 1 and week 8.

title: Uniswap User Origination
text:
We learned that

Uniswap Frontend onboarded the most trader to Uniswap, account for 55 traders in every 100 new traders of Uniswap.
MEV contributes the most to total Uniswap volume, account for $42 in every $100 of total Uniswap volume.
About a third of total traders, onboarded by each route, are multi-route traders.
Of multi-route traders, two third became multi-route in the first nine weeks (week 0-8).
Traders who started Uniswap Frontend or MEV tend to become multi-route traders later than those started with the other routes. In other words, traders who started Uniswap Frontend or MEV tend to stick longer to their first route than those started with the other routes.

title: Uniswap User Origination
section: What type of trader, new or return, drive the growth of the routes?
text:
What type of trader, new or return, drive the growth of the routes?
Traders, who are new to a route, not necessarily new to Uniswap, are the new traders of the route.
On average, every week, for every 100 traders, the new traders of the routes range between 13 (MEV) and 43 (Aggregators and Unknowns). This implies return traders play a more important role in driving the routes growth.
An other point is despite similar growth rate in volume and number of trader, share of new trader of MEV is only a third of those of other routes.


In case you are not familiar with retention rate, you can read more about it here.
In the first 106 weeks (i.e. the first 2 years), with the first week is week 0:

In week 1, retention rate of MEV drop to 48%, while of other routes drop to between 15% and 23%. After that, retention rate of MEV is minimum double, usually triple those of other routes. This offsets the difference in new trader share we learned above, which affects the routes growth.
Trailing behind MEV is Uniswap Frontend, which retention rate flat above 4%.
After week 100, Aggregators retention rate drop to as low as 2%, and Unknowns rentention rate drop to sub 1%.

title: Uniswap User Origination
text:
We learned that

In volume, Uniswap Frontend has the fastest weekly growth rate, while Aggregators and MEV have the slowest weekly growth rate.
In number of new trader onboarded to Uniswap, Unknowns has the fastest weekly growth rate, while MEV has the slowest weekly growth rate.
Return traders mainly drive the routes’ growth, especially in MEV.
Weekly retention rate of Uniswap Frontend is usually double of those of Aggregators and Unknowns, while only a third of that of MEV.

title: Uniswap User Origination
text:
We learned that

Volume per week of normal traders of Uniswap Frontend is a fifteenth of that of MEV, and more than triple those of the other 3 routes.
Volume per week of large traders of Uniswap Frontend is less than one hundredth of that of MEV, similar to that of Aggregators, 7 folds of that of Oku, and a third of that of Unknowns.
Number of trade per week of normal traders of Uniswap Frontend is two third of that of MEV and double those of the other routes.
Number of trade per week of large traders of Uniswap Frontend is a fifteenth of that of MEV, a third of that of Unknowns, quite similar to that of Aggreagtors, and more than double that of Oku.

title: Uniswap User Origination
text:
Chain breakdown
Uniswap Frontend volume shares range between 13% (BNB) and 39% (Arbitrum).
Aggregators volume shares range between 9% (Arbitrum) and 16% (Optimism).

text:

Version Breakdown

In the first year, i.e. first 53 weeks, volume of v3 grew faster than that of v2. However, in the second year, volume of v2 grew faster than that of v3. After the first 2 years, volume of v3 surpassed that of v2 again. At week 117 (i.e. 118 weeks since v3 launched, as of July 30, 2023), v3 volume is 9% higher than that of v2.




Contract Breakdown
Uniswap Frontend has 6 groups of contract, number of contract of the contract groups range between 1 (Uniswap_v2: Router01, Uniswap_v2: Router02, and Uniswap_v3: Router) and 1.8K (Uniswap_v1: Exchange).


Volume of the contract groups range between $531M+ (Uniswap_v1: Exchange) and $206B+ (Uniswap_v2: Router02). For every $100 in total Uniswap Frontend volume, $44 in Uniswap_v2: Router02, $23 in Uniswap_v3: SwapRouter02, $22 in Uniswap_v3: Router, $11 in Uniswap_v3: UniversalRouter, the other 2 contract groups volume shares are tiny.

For every 100 new trader, 41 in Uniswap_v2: Router02, 27 in Uniswap_v3: SwapRouter02, 20 in Uniswap_v3: UniversalRouter, 11 in Uniswap_v3: Router, 1 in the other 2 contract groups.














Traders who trade on Uniswap Frontend among other routes in each route
For every 100 traders who started with Uniswap Frontend, 26 traders also trade on other routes. These 26 traders account for $71 in $100 volume of the 100 traders. These traders are multi-route traders we examined above.
For every 100 traders who started with Aggregators, 19 traders also trade on Uniswap Frontend. These 19 traders account for $65 in $100 volume of the 100 traders.
For every 100 traders who started with MEV, 19 traders also trade on Uniswap Frontend. These 19 traders account for $15 in $100 volume of the 100 traders.
For every 100 traders who started with Unknowns, 14 traders also trade on Uniswap Frontend. These 14 traders account for $22 in $100 volume of the 100 traders.
All traders, who trade on Oku, also trade on Uniswap Frontend.




Traders who prefer Uniswap Frontend to other routes in each route
A trader, who prefers to trade on Uniswap Frontend, trades more either in volume or in number of trade on Uniswap Frontend than on the other routes in the period starting from the first trade on Uniswap Frontend (of traders who did not became multi-route with Uniswap Fronted) or the first trade on the orther routes (of traders who started with Uniswap Frontend).
Other than Oku, more than half of traders who trade on Uniswap Frontend of the routes prefer Uniswap Frontend.
Other than Uniswap Frontend, each route has more traders, who prefer Uniswap Frontend in volume than in number of trade. Uniswap Frontend has more traders, who prefer Uniswap Frontend, in number of trade than in volume.
Oku does not appear on the chart because none of The Traders returned to trade on Oku after trading on Uniswap Frontend.

title: Uniswap User Origination
text:
Oku
With limit order, there are 3 actions a trader can do on Oku advance trading interface: place New order, Cancel a not-yet-filled order, and Claim a filled order.

A trader starts by placing a limit order, which calls the function newOrder of the LimitOrderRegistry contract deployed by Oku, which returns the NewOrder event.
If the order is not filled, the trader can cancel the order, which calls the newOrder function, which returns the CancelOrder event.
If the order is filled, which returns the OrderFilled event, then the trader can claim the order, which calls the claimOrder function, which returns the ClaimOrder event.

If you are familiar with developing smart contract on Ethereum, you can read more about the contract code here. The diagram below roughly demonstrates the whole process.




flowchart LR
    A[New Order] --> B{Order Filled?}
    B -->|No| C(Cancel Order)
    B -->|Yes| D(Claim Order)





Volume of the chains range between $718 (Optimism) and $94K (Arbitrum).


Trader onboarded to Oku by the chains range between 2 (Ethereum) and 19 (Arbitrum).


For every $100 in Oku volume, $97 in Arbitrum, $2 in Ethereum, and $1 in Optimism.


32 traders placed $97K+ volume (NewOrder), 18 of 32 traders canceled $14K+, 14 of 32 traders claimed $3.6K out of $4.4K of total volume of filled orders, which means only 4.5% of total volume has been filled.












Of 32 traders, Oku only onboarded 2 traders to Uniswap.


Of 32 traders of Oku, 10 traders trade more on Oku, in average volume per trade, than on the other routes.



Do traders pay less gas fee on Oku than on the other routes?
To be compatible to gas fee of the other routes, gas fee of Oku is total of gas fee of (1) placing order (NewOrder), (2) filling order (OrderFilled), and (3) claiming filled order (ClaimOrder). I analyze period from the first Oku first transaction to exclude ETH price movement effect to gas fee in the prior Oku period.
Yes, traders pay less gas fee on Oku than on the other routes. Average Gas Fee per Trade of the routes range between $2.5 (Oku) and $35.9 (MEV).
Other than MEV, gas fee of Uniswap Frontend is higher than the other routes. Oku gas fee is less than a third of that of Uniswap Frontend.

title: Uniswap User Origination
text:
Unknowns
In Unknowns route, I identify 2 contracts of 2 market makers, which are 0x9507c04b10486547584c37bcbd931b2a4fee9a41 of Jump Trading and 0x00000000ae347930bd1e7b0f35588b92280f9e75 of Wintermute.
The 2 contracts account for $11 in every $100 of total Unknowns volume, 8 trades in every 1000 trades of total Unknowns trades.












Volume per trade of the 2 market makers range between $0.6 and $3.2M, and the mid 50%, i.e. IQR, range between $1.7K (TP25) and $47K+ (TP75).


I use the mid 50% range above as thresholds in an attemp to categorize the Unknowns route. If a contract’s TP75 is smaller than $1.7K then it is Not a market maker. If its TP25 > $1.7K, it is likely to be a market maker. Otherwise it is not identified, i.e. Others. Then I combine the 2 market makers with the likely to be a market maker into one as Market makers and alikes.
Volume of the contract groups range between $18B+ (Not market maker) and $161B+ (Market makers and alikes).


For every $100 of total Unknowns volume, $69 in Market makers and alikes, $23 in Others, and $8 in Not market maker.


Trader who started with the contract groups range between 8K+ (Market maker and alikes) and 945K+ (Not market maker).


For every 100 traders started with the Unknowns route, 95 in Not market maker, 4 in Others, and 1 in Market makers and alikes.


Number of trade of the contract groups range between 7M+ (Marketmaker and alikes) and 40M+ (Not market maker).


For every 100 trades, 64 in Not market maker, 25 in Others, and 11 in Market makers and alikes.

title: Crypto Winter Usage of Uniswap
text:
Uniswap launched on November 2, 2018. In July 2021, Uniswap started expanding to other chains. At the end of August 2023, on the trading side, Uniswap had 11.9m in total traders, who traded $1.6t in total volume. On the providing liquidity side, Uniswap had 1.2m in total liquidity providers, who added and removed liquidity, resulting in a net liquidity of $3.8b.


In this post, you will learn about Uniswap’s usage, of both trading and providing liquidity, on a monthly basis from May 2021, when Uniswap’s total monthly volume reached its all-time high, to August 2023, i.e. this crypto winter. Then I’ll dive deeper into Uniswap’s usage on Ethereum, the first and largest chain of Uniswap, both on a monthly basis and at the end of August 2023 snapshot.

title: Crypto Winter Usage of Uniswap
text:
Uniswap launched on November 2, 2018. In July 2021, Uniswap started expanding to other chains. At the end of August 2023, on the trading side, Uniswap had 11.9m in total traders, who traded $1.6t in total volume. On the providing liquidity side, Uniswap had 1.2m in total liquidity providers, who added and removed liquidity, resulting in a net liquidity of $3.8b.


In this post, you will learn about Uniswap’s usage, of both trading and providing liquidity, on a monthly basis from May 2021, when Uniswap’s total monthly volume reached its all-time high, to August 2023, i.e. this crypto winter. Then I’ll dive deeper into Uniswap’s usage on Ethereum, the first and largest chain of Uniswap, both on a monthly basis and at the end of August 2023 snapshot.

title: Crypto Winter Usage of Uniswap
text:
2 Key takeaways

On a monthly basis, from May 2021, when Uniswap monthly volume reached its all-time high, to August 2023, both Uniswap’s monthly volume and end-of-month TVL dropped more than 50% from all-time highs. Meanwhile, monthly active traders and monthly active LPs increased significantly, 80% or more versus the numbers in May 2021, thanks to the expansions to other chains, started in July 2021.
On a monthly basis, from May 2021 to August 2023, on Ethereum-based Uniswap:


About trading:

New traders contributed more than return traders in the recovery of number of traders after June 2022.
Uniswap v2 was the main driver behind the fall and rise of number of new trader.
M-1 return traders, who returned after being active in the previous month, was the main driver behind the decrease, before June 2022, and the recovery of number of return trader, after June 2022. Interestingly, number of M-7 or longer return traders, who returned after being dormant 7 months or longer, was also increasing, contributed to the recovery of return traders.

About providing liquidity:

Before June 2022, total monthly active LPs were down, number of return LPs was larger than that of new LPs. After June 2022, total monthly active LPs started to recover, number of return LPs was smaller than that of new LPs. During the whole period, number of return LPs dropped faster on the way down, and recovered slower on the way up than that of new LPs.
Uniswap v2 was the main driver behind the fall and rise of number of new LPs.
M-1 return LPs, who returned after being active in the previous month, was the main driver behind the fall and rise of number of return LPs.



At the end of August 2023, during 12 months started in September 2022, on Ethereum-based Uniswap:


About trading:

There were more dormant traders than active traders.
If traders were active in August 2023, 23% of them were still active on other DEXs (including DEXs on Ethreum and other chains). That number increases to 30% if they were dormant in August 2023, drops to 12% if they were dormant for 13-24 months, drops to 6% if they were dormant for more than 24 months.
5 most favorite Ethereum-based DEXs of traders after their last trade on Ethereum-based Uniswap were Sushiswap, 0x API, airswap, curve, shibaswap.
5 most favorite other-chains-based DEXs of traders after their last trade on Ethereum-based Uniswap were pancakeswap, sushiswap, quickswap, camelot, trader_joe.

About providing liquidity:

There were more dormant LPs than active LPs.
There were more LPs with at least one open position than LPs with no open position.
There were more LPs, who never closed any position than LPs, who closed at least one position.
The longer LPs being dormant, the less likely that they gained from providing liquidity. If LPs were dormant more than 12 months, the longer being dormant, the more likely that they had lost from providing liquidity.

title: Crypto Winter Usage of Uniswap
text:
3 Uniswap, monthly
This section has 4 sub-sections. The first 2 sub-sections are about trading and providing liquidity. The third sub-section tried to answer the question: what would have happened if Uniswap had not expanded to other chains? The last sub-section summarizes the first three sub-sections.

3.1 Trading

3.1.1 Volume

In May 2021, Uniswap’s total monthly volume (the number) reached its all-time high, reaching $90.8b. Since July 2021, when Uniswap started expanding to other chains, Ethereum-based Uniswap’s share in the number reached its all-time low in April 2023, reaching 63.5%.
In August 2023, the number was $25.9b, reflecting a 71% drop from the all-time high. Meanwhile, Ethereum-based Uniswap’s share in the number was 76.1%, a 12.6% increase from the all-time low.





3.1.2 Traders

Uniswap’s total number of traders (the number) reached its all-time high in May 2023, reaching 1.8m, a 80% increase from May 2021. Meanwhile, Ethereum-based Uniswap’s share in the number decreased rapidly, reached its all-time low in June 2023, reaching 32.2%.
In August 2023, the number was 1.4m, reflecting a 22% drop from the all-time high. Meanwhile, Ethereum-based Uniswap’s share in the number was 38.8%, a 6.6% increase from the all-time low.






3.2 Providing liquidity

3.2.1 TVL
Monthly TVL is calculated at end of month.

Uniswap’s monthly TVL (the number) reached its all-time high in November 2021, reaching $8.7b. Since then, it gradually decreased to $3.8b in December 2022. Since then, it ranged between $3.8b and $4.9b. Meanwhile, Ethereum-based Uniswap’s share in the number was declining, but has never dropped below 89%.
At the end of August 2023, the number was $3.8b, reflecting a 56% drop from the all-time high. Meanwhile, Ethereum-based Uniswap’s share in the number was 91.2%.





3.2.2 LPs

The trends of Uniswap’s total monthly active LPs (the number) and Ethereum-based Uniswap’s share in the number were quite opposite to each other. In April 2023, the number reached its all-time high, reaching 119.5k, a 85% increase from May 2021. And Ethereum-based Uniswap’s share in the number reached its all-time low, reaching 37.1%.
In August 2023, the number was 74k, reflecting a 38% drop from the all-time high. Meanwhile, Ethereum-based Uniswap’s share in the number was 56.1%, a 19% increase from the all-time low.






3.3 What if Uniswap had not expanded to other chains?
If Uniswap had not expanded to other chains, in 2023:

total monthly volume would have been 16%-37% lower,
total monthly TVL would have been 6%-10% lower,
total monthly active traders would have been 43%-68% lower,
total monthly active LPs would have been 42%-63% lower,

In short, the impact of Uniswap not expanding to other chains would have been non-trivial, especially on total monthly active traders and LPs.




3.4 Summary
From May 2021 to August 2023, both monthly volume and TVL dropped significantly from the all-time highs, 71% and 56% respectively. Meanwhile, monthly active traders and monthly active LPs grew significantly, thanks to the expansions to other chains. Without the expansions, they would have been declining, not growing.
In the next sections, let’s dive deeper into Uniswap usage on Ethereum.

title: Crypto Winter Usage of Uniswap
text:
4 Ethereum-based Uniswap, monthly
This section has three sub-sections. The first sub-section is about trading. The second sub-section is about providing liquidity. The last sub-section summarizes the first two sub-sections.

4.1 Trading

4.1.1 New and return in total
After May 2021:

In absolute number of change, Uniswap’s total monthly volume dropped sharply 2 months after its all-time high, mostly in volume of return traders, which has never accounted for less than 71%. In rate of change, total monthly volume of both new and return traders were down at similar rates. However, in 2023, total monthly volume of new traders dropped slower than that of return traders, reflected in downward slope of the percentage of volume of return traders in total monthly volume.




Uniswap’s monthly active new traders has never dropped below 100k. It bottomed at 101.3k in June 2022. It usually lead 1 month the trend of monthly active return traders. The latter bottomed at 224k in July 2022. Since then, the two trends were generally up. However, number of monthly active new traders were increasing faster than number of monthly active return traders, reflected in downward slope of the percentage of return traders in total monthly active traders.




Both monthly volume per trader of new and return traders were down generally. Monthly volume per return trader was usually 4x-10x larger than that of new trader. In 2023, though monthly volume per trader of both groups were down, the gap was smaller than in the prior years, which indicates that monthly volume per return traders dropped faster than that of new traders.





4.1.2 New trader

Uniswap’s total monthly active new traders correlated the most with Uniswap v2’s new traders, as the latter accounted for at least 51% of the former monthly.





4.1.3 Return trader

Uniswap’s total monthly active return traders correlated the most with M-1 return traders, who returned after trading in the previous month. Except for January 2023, M-1 return traders has never accounted for less than half of total monthly active return traders.
Interestingly, number of M-7 or longer return traders, who returned after being dormant for 7 months or longer, had been increasing and peaked in May 2023.




The main driver of the rise of M-7 or longer return were a subset of return traders, who returned after being dormant for 7-24 months, which consist of 2 smaller subsets: M-7 - M-12 and M-13 - M-24. Interestingly, the latter were smaller than the former in all months except for May 2023, when the latter peaked at 9.9% while the former were 7.7%.






4.2 Providing liquidity
This section has three sub-sections. The first sub-section is about contribution of new and return in total LPs. The last two sub-sections dive deeper into new and return LPs.

4.2.1 New and return in total
After May 2021:

Both monthly number of new and return LPs had been down until June 2022. Since then, both numbers were up.
Meanwhile, the percentage of return LPs in total monthly active LPs kept decreasing during the whole period. In other words, number of return LPs dropped faster on the way down, and recovered slower on the way up than that of new LPs.





4.2.2 New LP

Uniswap’s total monthly active new LPs correlated the most with Uniswap v2’s new LPs, as the latter accounted for at least 58% of the former monthly.





4.2.3 Return LP

Uniswap’s total monthly active return LPs correlated the most with M-1 return LPs, who returned after trading in the previous month. M-1 return LPs is the largest subset, accounted for at least 44% of total monthly active return LPs.
Similar to but not as clear as number of M-7 or longer return traders, number of M-7 or longer return LPs, who returned after being dormant for 7 months or longer, had been increasing and peaked in February 2023.




The group of M-7 - M-12 return LPs was the driver of the rise of M-7 or longer return LPs in 2022. Recently, number of M-25 or longer return LPs was increasing.






4.3 Summary
From May 2021 to August 2023, Ethereum-based Uniswap monthly performance:

Total monthly volume and total monthly active traders were generally down. Before June 2022, volume dropped faster than number of trader. After June 2022, volume kept dropping, but slower, while number of traders started to recover. New traders contributed more than return traders in the recovery of number of trader. Uniswap v2 was the main driver behind the fall and rise of number of new trader. Meanwhile, M-1 return traders, who returned after being active in the previous month, was the main driver behind the fall and rise of number of return trader. While other subsets of return traders fell then rose, M-7 or longer return traders, who returned after being dormant for 7 months or longer, kept rising during most of the period.
Before June 2022, total monthly active LPs were down, number of return LPs was larger than that of new LPs. After June 2022, total monthly active LPs started to recover, number of return LPs was smaller than that of new LPs. During the whole period, number of return LPs dropped faster on the way down, and recovered slower on the way up than that of new LPs. Similar to the trading side, Uniswap v2 was the main driver behind the fall and rise of number of new LPs. Meanwhile M-1 return LPs, who returned after being active in the previous month, was the main driver behind the fall and rise of number of return LPs.

title: Crypto Winter Usage of Uniswap
text:
5 Ethereum-based Uniswap’s traders, August 2023 snapshot
This section has 4 sub-sections. The first sub-section is about all traders. The next 2 sub-sections are about traders who were active and dormant between September 2022 and August 2023. The last sub-section summarizes the first 3 sub-sections.

5.1 All traders
From November 2018, when Uniswap launched, to August 2023:

Most of Uniswap’s traders have their last trade after August 2020.
To at least 45% of LPs, who have their last trade in a given month, that last month is also their first month. August 2023 is excluded in this number as it is the last observed month.



At the end of August 2023, 7.5m traders had traded on Uniswap on Ethereum, out of every 100 traders:

59 traders were dormant, i.e. did not trade, between September 2022 and August 2023, i.e. Dormant 12 months traders,
41 traders were active, i.e. traded at least once, between September 2022 and August 2023, i.e. Active 12 months traders.



Next, let’s dive deeper into 2 groups of traders above.


5.2 Traders, active between September 2022 and August 2023
3.1m traders were active between September 2022 and August 2023, out of every 100 traders:

82 traders were dormant in August 2023,
18 traders were active in August 2023.




5.2.1 What percentage of each group active elsewhere?
Traders who were active on Uniswap between September 2022 and August 2023:

At the end of August 2023, 927k LPs had provided liquidity on Uniswap on Ethereum:

56% of LPs were dormant, i.e. did not add or remove liquidity, and 44% of LPs were active, i.e. added or removed liquidity at least once, between September 2022 and August 2023.




61% of LPs have at least 1 open position, and 39% of LPs have no open position.

43 LPs were dormant 13-24 months.





6.4 Gain, loss from providing liquidity
At the end of August 2023, on all 3 versions of Ethereum-based Uniswap, out of every 100 LPs:

51 LPs never completely removed (closed) any position.

Out of every 10 LPs, who have closed at least 1 position, 4 LPs have price data to calculate their PnL,




Out of every 100 LPs who have PnL calculated, 65 LPs gained, 25 LPs lost, 10 LPs were break even from providing liquidity,




When diving deeper into these LPs, 2 parterns emerge:

The percentage of LPs who had gained from providing liquidity of a subset decreases as the longer LPs of the subset being dormant. In other words, the longer LPs being dormant, the less likely that they had gained from providing liquidity.
In subsets which LPs were dormant more than 12 months, the percentage of LPs who had lost from providing liquidity of a subset increases as the longer LPs of the subset being dormant. In other words, if LPs were dormant more than 12 months, the longer being dormant, the more likely that they had lost from providing liquidity.

title: Uniswap V3 Impermanent Loss on Ethereum
text:
Liquidity Providers (LPs) deposit assets into Uniswap’s pools to earn trading fee paid by traders. It’s an investing strategy. Let’s call the strategy LPing.
During 60 days from July 1 to August 29, 2023 (the 60 days period), Uniswap V3 TVL on Ethereum ranged between $2.3B and $2.9B. TVL was trending down from $2.8B to $2.4B.


What can they do with the assets instead? The simplest stategy is to hold them, let’s call the stragtegy Hodling.
Compare to Hodling, the upside of LPing is earning fee. What is the downside? It’s the loss of assets value, i.e. impermanent loss (IL).
Uniswap document has a section to understand more about IL, which you can read here.
In this post, let’s explore IL of LPing in Uniswap V3.

title: Uniswap V3 Impermanent Loss on Ethereum
text:
Uniswap V3
During the 60 days period, on average, IL of Uniswap V3 ranged between $59K and $1.1M per day. % IL of Uniswap V3 ranged between 0.002% and 0.042% per day.

title: Uniswap V3 Impermanent Loss on Ethereum
text:
Pool breakdown
Pool name consists of [Base symbol-Quote symbol : % Fee].
During the 60 days period, Uniswap V3 had 15K+ pools. Out of every 100 pools, 92 pools had [positive] reserve [in all 60 days], 6 pools didn’t have [zero] reserve, and 2 pools had questionable [?] reserve (negative reserve, might due to wrong data).


In 14K+ Positive reserve pools, 2.9K+ pools had price data. Out of every 100 pools in those 2.9K pools, 60 pools had % IL ranged between 0.002% and 0.04%, 31 pools had % IL greater than 0.04%, 9 pools had % IL less than 0.002%.



Top TVL and volume pools
Average daily IL of top 8 TVL pools ranged between $1.7 (USDC-USDT : 1%, top stablecoins pool) and $15.9K (USDC-WETH : 0.05%, the largest TVL pool).


% IL of most of the pools is either 0.001% or 0.004%. Top pools, with 0.004% IL, all consist of WETH and stablecoins. Top pools, with 0.001% IL, all consist of WETH and WBTC.




The top TVL pools are also the top volume pools.






Top IL pools
Average daily IL of top IL pools ranged between $10.1K (USDC-UST : 1%) and $63.2K (KUMA-WETH : 1%). 3 of these pools are the top TVL pools.


Meanwhile, % IL ranged between 0.004% (USDC-WETH : 0.3%) and 40.911% (KUMA-WETH : 1%). % IL of 7 pools were around 5% or less.




If considering % IL of Uniswap V3 as the % IL among all pools, take the highest % IL per day we learned above at 0.042%, % IL of the top 8 IL pools were 40x-1700x higher than average. LPs should think carefully before LPing into these pools.


Top Profitability [% IL + % fee] pools
Let’s add trading fee into the equation to see which pool generate the most profit from $1 of liquidity, i.e. % IL + % Fee.
The top profitability pools have % IL + % Fee ranged between 21.2% (WBTC-VERSE : 0.3%) and 81.5% (PEPE-WETH : 0.05%).




Average daily profit of top profit pools ranged between $0.1 (WETH-HAKA : 0.05%) and $7.3K (PEPE-WETH : 0.05%). All of these pools had very small TVL, i.e. long-tail pools.

title: Uniswap V3 Impermanent Loss on Ethereum
text:
Position breakdown
In Uniswap V3, a position is a combination of a LP, a pool, and a range of price to provide liquidity, which has 2 boundaries, Price Lower and Price Upper.
In 46K+ positions openned during the 60 days period, out of every 100 positions, 39 positions had no burn (hadn’t removed any provided liquidity), 28 positions were closed intraday, 17 positions were closed after multi-weeks [since the first mint], 13 positions were closed after 2-7 days, and 3 positions had partially removed but not yet closed.


In 12K+ intraday positions, out of every 100 positions, 42 positions were closed in different hour [from first mint], 16 positions were closed in the same hour, and 42 positions were JIT, i.e. closed intrablock.


Next, let’s examine No burn positions, the largest subset.

No burn positions
In total 17K+ No burn positions, 4K+ didn’t have price data. In the other 13K+ positions, out of every 100 positions, 11 positions didn’t have IL, IL of 88 postions were $100 or less.



Top TVL positions
IL of top 8 positions ranged between $0 and $19.7K. IL of other 13K positions were $200K+.


With TVL when mint, i.e. Hodling value, ranged from $9.5m to $35.9m, % IL of top 8 positions ranged from 0% to 0.169%.






Top IL positions
IL of top 8 positions ranged between $3K and $63K. The largest IL position and 4 others were in meme pools, i.e. pools consist of meme coins and WETH. The other 3 positions were in the largest TVL pool USDC-WETH : 0.05%.


With TVL when mint, i.e. Hodling value, ranged from $76.4k to $6.8m, % IL of top 8 positions ranged from 0.020% to 4.889%. % IL of other 13K positions were 0.038%. % IL of positions in pool USDC-WETH : 0.05% were much smaller than those of positions in meme pools.
